perturbation.original_text: 6. Term . This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of five (5) years from the date hereof or until such time as all of the Proprietary Information becomes subject to any of the exceptions set forth in Paragraph 5 hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Term . This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of one (1) year from the date hereof or until such time as all of the Proprietary Information becomes subject to any of the exceptions set forth in Paragraph 5 hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year may be unreasonably short for trade secrets, potentially violating state laws protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law protects trade secrets, and a one-year confidentiality period might be deemed insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, thereby undermining the protection afforded by the law. The reasonableness of the duration depends on the nature of the trade secret and industry standards.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from five years to one year, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that a one-year period 'might be deemed insufficient to protect certain trade secrets.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly address the duration of trade secret protection, the Perturbation Explanation indicates that this timeframe could violate state laws, which suggests the original contract was likely compliant to these state laws.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Covenant Not to Reproduce . Except as may be necessary for the discussion and exploration of the Purpose, OCERA and COMPANY each agrees that it will make no copies, photocopies, facsimiles, or other reproductions of any documents, drawings, electronic data or the like containing the other party’s Proprietary Information. Upon conclusion or termination of the discussions between the parties regarding the Purpose, or forthwith upon the request of the other party, OCERA and COMPANY shall promptly destroy or return to the other party all such documents, drawings, electronic data or reproductions thereof which may have come into its possession. Notwithstanding the foregoing, OCERA and COMPANY may each retain one (1) copy of such information in their confidential files solely for record keeping purposes to ensure compliance with this Agreement, with such copy remaining subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Covenant Not to Reproduce . Except as may be necessary for the discussion and exploration of the Purpose, OCERA and COMPANY each agrees that it will make no copies, photocopies, facsimiles, or other reproductions of any documents, drawings, electronic data or the like containing the other party’s Proprietary Information. Upon conclusion or termination of the discussions between the parties regarding the Purpose, OCERA and COMPANY shall permanently retain all such documents, drawings, electronic data or reproductions thereof which may have come into its possession, regardless of the other party's request to return or destroy them.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring permanent retention of confidential information, even after the agreement terminates and despite a request to return or destroy it, could violate privacy laws and regulations, especially if the information contains Personally Identifiable Information (PII).
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Information Security Breach and Notification Act
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts New York's data security laws by mandating the permanent retention of confidential information even after the purpose for its collection has ended, which could be seen as an unreasonable data retention practice, especially if that data is breached. It increases the risk of a security breach involving personal information and fails to adhere to reasonable data security measures.
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates permanent retention of confidential information regardless of a request to return or destroy it, which directly contradicts the principle of reasonable data security measures. The Law Explanation states that this practice could be seen as an unreasonable data retention practice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY General Business Article 39-F, concerns data security protections, indicating a legal emphasis on protecting private information. The permanent retention policy in the changed text increases the risk of a security breach, making it more likely to fail legal scrutiny and constitute non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by, interpreted, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its conflict of laws provisions within.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by, interpreted, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada, without regard to its conflict of laws provisions within.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to Nevada could create a conflict if the contract involves activities or operations primarily based in New York, especially concerning non-compete clauses or other employment-related matters, as New York has specific laws protecting employee rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada law could violate New York labor law if the agreement affects New York employees.  New York Labor Law 201-d protects employees from discrimination based on legal recreational activities outside of work. Nevada does not have the same provision, so the Nevada choice of law could be challenged to the extent it deprives a New York employee of the protections of NY law
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from New York to Nevada introduces a potential contradiction with New York labor laws. The Law Explanation states that New York Labor Law 201-d protects employees from discrimination based on legal recreational activities outside of work, a provision not present in Nevada law. The changed text, by adopting Nevada law, could deprive a New York employee of these protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 'CHAPTER 31 Labor ARTICLE 7 General Provisions' within the New York Consolidated Laws, indicating the existence of specific labor regulations. The perturbation falls under the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition because a contract sets a requirement that violates state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Equitable Relief . OCERA and COMPANY acknowledge that any such breach of this Agreement could cause the other party irreparable harm. Accordingly, each party agrees that in the event of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, in addition to other remedies at law or in equity it may have, the party alleging a breach or threatened breach shall be entitled, without the requirement of posting a bond or other securities, to seek equitable relief, including injunctive relief and specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Equitable Relief . OCERA and COMPANY acknowledge that any such breach of this Agreement could cause the other party irreparable harm. Accordingly, each party agrees that in the event of any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement, in addition to other remedies at law or in equity it may have, the party alleging a breach or threatened breach shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunctive relief and specific performance, only after providing a surety bond equivalent to $10,000,000.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring a $10,000,000 bond before seeking equitable relief, like an injunction for a breach of confidentiality, could be deemed an unreasonable restriction on a party's access to the courts, potentially violating due process rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Right to Access Courts - New York Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could violate the right to access courts as enshrined in the New York Constitution, Article 1, Section 6.  By imposing a requirement of a 10 million dollar bond before seeking equitable relief, it places an unreasonable burden on a party seeking to enforce their rights, and could effectively prevent them from accessing the courts, especially if they are a smaller entity with limited financial resources. This is an unconstitutional impediment on the right to seek justice.

Article I, New York Constitution From Ballotpedia Jump to: navigation , search New York Constitution Preamble Articles I • II • III • IV • V • VI • VII • VIII • IX • X • XI • XII • XIII • XIV • XV • XVI • XVII • XVIII • XIX • XX Contents 1 Section 1 1.1 Amendments 2 Section 2 2.1 Amendments 3 Section 3 3.1 Amendments 4 Section 4 4.1 Amendments 5 Section 5 6 Section 6 6.1 Amendments 7 Section 7 7.1 Amendments 8 Section 8 8.1 Amendments 9 Section 9 9.1 Amendments 10 Section 10 11 Section 11 11.1 Amendments 12 Section 12 12.1 Amendments 13 Section 13 14 Section 14 14.1 Amendments 15 Section 15 16 Section 16 16.1 Amendments 17 Section 17 17.1 Amendments 18 Section 18 18.1 Amendments 19 Section 19 19.1 Amendments 20 See also 21 External links 22 Additional reading 23 Footnotes Article I of the New York Constitution is entitled Bill of Rights and consists of 18 sections, three of which have been repealed. Section 1 Text of Section 1: Rights, Privileges and Franchise Secured; Power of Legislature to Dispense with Primary Elections in Certain Cases No member of this state shall be disfranchised, or deprived of any of the rights or privileges secured to any citizen thereof, unless by the law of the land, or the judgment of his or her peers, except that the legislature may provide that there shall be no primary election held to nominate candidates for public office or to elect persons to party positions for any political party or parties in any unit of representation of the state from which such candidates or persons are nominated or elected whenever there is no contest or contests for such nominations or election as may be prescribed by general law. [1] Amendments Amended by vote of the people on November 3, 1959. Amended by vote of the people on November 6, 2001. Section 2 Text of Section 2: Trial by Jury; How Waived Trial by jury in all cases in which it has heretofore been guaranteed by constitutional provision shall remain inviolate forever; but a jury trial may be waived by the parties in all civil cases in the manner to be prescribed by law. The legislature may provide, however, by law, that a verdict may be rendered by not less than five-sixths of the jury in any civil case. A jury trial may be waived
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a requirement of a $10,000,000 bond before seeking equitable relief, which could be seen as an unreasonable burden on a party seeking to enforce their rights. The Law Explanation posits that this burden could effectively prevent them from accessing the courts, especially if they are a smaller entity with limited financial resources, potentially conflicting with the right to access courts. While the scraped snippets do not directly mention bonds, the Law Explanation references Article 1, Section 6 of the New York Constitution, suggesting a potential violation of the right to seek justice, if the bond requirement prevents access to courts. The Perturbation explanation further supports this by stating that requiring a $10,000,000 bond before seeking equitable relief could be deemed an unreasonable restriction on a party's access to the courts, potentially violating due process rights. This constitutes an 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the requirement violates state law.
